headlines.geopolitics: Community honors 'unclaimed' Navy veteran with full military burial in Tennessee
headlines.economy: DOJ launches new fraud division targeting billions in benefit fraud
headlines.domestic: Americans expect AI to transform society but find it mostly useless today

metadata.daily_do: Check if your local VA cemetery has unclaimed veterans scheduled for burial. Most welcome community attendance to ensure no veteran is buried alone.

clips.caption: When Duke gets emotional about veterans being forgotten
Pringle drops historical knowledge about honoring unknown soldiers
Jax roasts AI hype with perfect timing

script.text: Look, I don't care if it costs four million dollars to investigate a four million dollar fraud. Some things aren't about the math.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction for Monday, March 10th. Three stories to start your week.

First, geopolitics. Community members and veterans gathered at Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery to provide full military honors for Navy veteran Lonnie D. Wayman, who had no known family to claim his remains. Hundreds attended to ensure he wasn't buried alone.
Oh man.
Good for them.

Second, economy. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division targeting widespread government benefit fraud. He cited cases including two Pennsylvania men who fraudulently received four million dollars for fake homeless shelters in Minneapolis.
Four million for fake homeless shelters? That's like, advanced evil.
While actual homeless people are still, you know, homeless.

Third, domestic. A new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters expect AI to significantly change American life, but only forty-seven percent find it useful in their daily lives currently. Voters overwhelmingly want AI-generated content clearly labeled.
So we're scared of the robot apocalypse but also like, can you help me write this email? No? Okay, never mind.
The gap between expectation and utility. Tale as old as time.
We'll start today with the story that, honestly, made me pause when I read it this morning. This veteran in Tennessee.

So Lonnie Wayman, Navy veteran, dies with no known family. Gets classified as 'unclaimed.' But instead of a quiet burial, hundreds of people show up. Veterans, community members, strangers who just heard about it.
The VA rep said something beautiful. He said the paperwork called him 'unclaimed' but that was wrong, because look at all these people claiming him now.
That's what I'm talking about. That's America right there.
I mean, it's beautiful, but it's also kind of heartbreaking that we need random strangers to show up because the system failed to track this guy's family.
That's the question, right? Is this a failure of the system, or is this the system working exactly as it should?
I think it's both? Like, the fact that hundreds of people showed up is incredible. But the fact that we have veterans dying alone with no family contact suggests we're not doing enough upstream.
Okay but here's the thing— sometimes people genuinely don't have family. Sometimes families fall apart. You can't bureaucracy your way out of human tragedy.
Sure, but we could do better tracking. The VA has his service record, right? They could cross-reference with Social Security, Medicare records, maybe find distant relatives or old battle buddies.
And spend how much money playing detective for every veteran who dies? Look, this story is beautiful precisely because it's community-driven. People chose to show up.
Can I just say, the phrase 'unclaimed veteran' is doing a lot of heavy lifting here? Like, unclaimed baggage is one thing. Unclaimed human being who served his country? That hits different.
The language matters. The VA rep clearly felt that too.
Right, and I'm not saying we need to assign a caseworker to every veteran. But like, basic outreach? Maybe annual check-ins for older vets who live alone?
You're talking about expanding government programs when what happened here is the opposite. This is civil society stepping up. Veteran groups, churches, regular people.
But Duke, civil society only works if people know about it. How many Lonnie Waymans are buried quietly because nobody organized a Facebook post?
And that's tragic. But you fix that with more community organizing, not more federal programs.
There's another angle here. The military creates bonds that civilian life doesn't always replicate. When you serve, you're part of something bigger.
Exactly. And that's why veterans showed up for Lonnie even though they never met him. Military service creates obligations that outlast your discharge date.
Which is beautiful, but also highlights the gap. What about veterans who don't connect with veteran groups? What about the ones who come home and just want to forget?
This is making me think about my grandfather. He was in Korea, never talked about it, died when I was twelve. I have no idea if he had veteran friends or not.
And that's common. A lot of guys from that generation just didn't talk about service. Didn't mean it didn't matter to them.
The ceremony ended with doves being released near the flagpoles. The rep said they were able to 'claim their honorable veteran and provide the dignity and honor he earned.'
God, that's going to make me cry.
Look, I don't care if it's community-driven or government-run. The outcome here is right. Nobody who wore the uniform should be buried alone if we can help it.
Agreed. Can we make that a thing? Like, can we set up some kind of alert system so people know when this is happening?
Actually, many VA cemeteries already do this. They post notices and welcome community attendance. It just doesn't always get the attention this one did.
So maybe the answer is better publicity, not bigger programs.
Now you're talking my language.
Professor, this story seems to touch on something deeper about how societies honor their military dead.

Indeed it does, Leo. This ceremony in Tennessee is part of a tradition that stretches back millennia. The impulse to ensure that those who served in war are not forgotten, even when their names are lost.
The most famous example, of course, is our own Tomb of the Unknown Soldier at Arlington. Established in nineteen twenty-one after the Great War, when so many bodies could not be identified.
Right, the guard who walks back and forth?
Twenty-one steps each direction, Jackson. Every hour of every day since nineteen thirty-seven. Through hurricanes, blizzards, the September eleventh attacks. Never abandoned.
That's incredible dedication.
But here's what's fascinating— the French did it first. Their Arc de Triomphe houses an unknown soldier from the Great War, with an eternal flame that's been burning since nineteen twenty-three.

The British followed suit with their Unknown Warrior in Westminster Abbey. The Soviets created their Tomb of the Unknown Soldier in Moscow. Even smaller nations— Belgium, Poland, Australia— all recognized this fundamental human need.
The need to honor sacrifice even when you don't know the name.

Precisely. But there's a deeper historical pattern here. In ancient Rome, soldiers who died far from home were often buried in mass graves by their comrades. The Romans believed that unburied soldiers would wander as restless spirits.
Medieval knights had the concept of 'crusader graves'— if you fell in the Holy Land, your brothers-in-arms would ensure proper burial, regardless of whether they knew your family back home.
So this has always been a thing? Soldiers taking care of soldiers?

Always. The American Civil War created our first national cemeteries because the scale of death overwhelmed local burial customs. Arlington itself was Robert E. Lee's property, confiscated and turned into a cemetery partly as punishment.

But what happened in Tennessee last week? That's not institutional. That's the ancient impulse asserting itself through modern community action. Social media where Roman soldiers had formal burial rites.
Same instinct, different tools.
Exactly. And historically, societies that lose this instinct— that allow their warriors to be forgotten— those societies tend to struggle with military recruitment and social cohesion.
Because if you don't honor the sacrifice, why would anyone make it?
Precisely. The contract between society and soldier includes the promise of remembrance. Break that promise, and the contract dissolves.
Which is why what happened in Tennessee matters beyond just one veteran.
Indeed. Those hundreds of attendees weren't just honoring Lonnie Wayman. They were renewing the contract for every service member watching.
Let's shift to the fraud story. Deputy AG Todd Blanche says the Justice Department is launching an entirely new division to combat government benefit fraud. He's calling the problem 'insane.'

Okay, so the specific case he mentions is pretty outrageous. Two guys from Pennsylvania drive to Minneapolis, sign up to run homeless shelters that don't exist, and walk away with four million dollars.
That's like the worst possible version of a road trip. 'Hey, want to drive to Minneapolis and steal money from homeless people?' 'Sure, sounds fun.'
And this is why people hate government spending. You've got actual homeless people on the streets while con artists are cashing four million dollar checks.
But Duke, that's an argument for better oversight, not less spending. The problem isn't that we're helping homeless people, it's that we're not verifying who we're sending the money to.
Agreed. But Blanche is talking about prosecuting anyone who steals over a thousand dollars. That's good. Make it clear there are consequences.
He also mentioned school lunch fraud. Fake daycare centers billing for kids that don't exist.
Which is particularly evil because it's literally taking food out of children's mouths. But here's my question— how much is this new division going to cost versus how much fraud it prevents?
That's always the calculation, right? But sometimes deterrence is worth the upfront cost.
Also, can we talk about how bad the current system is if two random guys from Philly can just drive to another state and successfully pretend to run homeless shelters?
Blanche said the government gives money away 'like candy' with no verification.
Which is maddening because there are legitimate organizations doing incredible work who have to jump through hoops to get funding.
Exactly. The good actors get buried in paperwork while the bad actors just walk off with millions. That's backwards.
I'm trying to imagine the conversation where these guys decided to become fake homeless shelter operators. Like, what's the business plan there?
Step one, exploit vulnerable populations. Step two, steal taxpayer money. Step three, profit. It's sociopathy with a business model.
And the fact that they got four million before anyone noticed suggests the oversight is essentially nonexistent.
The question is whether aggressive prosecution actually deters fraud or just creates more overcrowding in federal prisons.
Look, I don't care if it costs four million dollars to investigate a four million dollar fraud. Some things aren't about the math.
I'm with you on prosecuting fraud. But we also need to fix the systems that make fraud this easy in the first place.
Agreed. Better verification upfront, harsh penalties on the back end.
Can I just say, if you're going to commit federal fraud, maybe don't drive from Pennsylvania to Minnesota to do it? Like, that's adding interstate transportation to your charges.
I think the FBI would like a word with your career counseling, Jax.
Alright, time for Rapid Fire. The weird news that makes you question reality. First up— a boss in Ireland repeatedly shouted the word 'potato' at his Irish employee as workplace harassment.
That's... wow. That's like cartoonishly offensive.
What year is this? Eighteen forty-seven?
The employee won the harassment case, by the way. Turns out shouting ethnic stereotypes at your workers is illegal. Who knew?
The potato famine killed over a million people. This is not humor, it's historical trauma.
Thank you, Professor. Next— Palantir's AI weapons system has been reassigned to find office chairs for government workers.
I'm sorry, what?
Apparently the same AI they built to identify enemy targets is now being used to locate ergonomic seating solutions in federal offices.
That's either the most expensive office furniture catalog in history or a really weird military strategy.
Your tax dollars at work, folks.
Moving on— Louisiana classrooms must display the Ten Commandments by this Friday's deadline.
Oh, this is going to be a constitutional law nightmare.
I mean, 'thou shalt not steal' seems relevant given our fraud discussion.
And finally, a Florida drug dealer locked his customer in a storage unit for months over unpaid debt.
For months?
Months. Like, this wasn't a Tuesday afternoon timeout. This was a full seasonal commitment to imprisonment.
That's kidnapping. That's very much kidnapping.
Florida continues to be Florida.
The storage unit company is probably like, 'Sir, your monthly payment is overdue.' 'Yeah, about that...'
Alright, let's wrap with our Daily Do. After that story about Lonnie Wayman, here's something you can actually act on.

Check if your local VA cemetery has any unclaimed veterans scheduled for burial. Most welcome community attendance to ensure no veteran is buried alone. It's usually posted on their website or you can call the cemetery office.
That's really beautiful, Leo. And it costs nothing but an hour of your time.
It's the kind of thing that restores your faith in people. Which we could all use more of.
As the Romans said, 'Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori.' It is sweet and proper to die for one's country. But I would add— it is equally proper to honor those who did.
And on that note, this has been The Friction. I'm Jax Mooney, reminding you that the news is weird, the world is complicated, but at least we're figuring it out together.
We'll see you tomorrow.
